description: Mater Amabilis Year 6 living books curriculum for grade 6. The Logic Stage is in full swing: Plutarch continues, American history moves toward the Republic, and literary analysis vocabulary begins alongside longer, more demanding novels.

books.title: Kim
books.author: Rudyard Kipling
books.subject: living_books
books.notes: An orphan boy moving between two worlds on the Grand Trunk Road. Rich material for discussing identity and loyalty. 'What does Kim have to choose between, and why is it hard?'

books.title: The Hobbit
books.author: J. R. R. Tolkien
books.subject: living_books
books.notes: A reluctant hero discovers courage he didn't know he had. 'How is Bilbo different at the end of the story than at the beginning, and what changed him?'

books.title: The Silver Chair
books.author: C. S. Lewis
books.subject: living_books
books.notes: The signs Aslan gives at the start matter more than what looks obvious later on — a good match for the Logic Stage's growing skepticism. 'Why is remembering the signs harder than trusting your own judgment in the moment?'

books.title: Plutarch's Lives (selected passages, continuing rotation)
books.author: Plutarch
books.subject: history
books.notes: Continuing the rotation. By this year the student should begin weighing competing virtues, not just narrating events. 'Which of this leader's qualities would you keep, which would you reject?'

books.title: The Story of the Great Republic
books.author: H. A. Guerber
books.subject: history
books.notes: Continues the American history arc from Year 5's colonies into the founding of the Republic. 'What problem was each founding decision trying to solve?'

books.title: Trial and Triumph: Stories from Church History (continuing)
books.author: Richard Hannula
books.subject: saints
books.notes: Continuing the rotation, now reaching the Reformation era in most editions. 'What was this person willing to lose for what they believed?'

books.title: Galileo and the Magic Numbers
books.author: Sidney Rosen
books.subject: science
books.notes: Galileo's life and the birth of observational, evidence-based science. 'Why was what Galileo saw through his telescope so dangerous to say out loud?'

books.title: Handbook of Nature Study (continuing reference)
books.author: Anna Botsford Comstock
books.subject: nature_study
books.notes: Continuing as a reference — consult for whatever the student is currently observing outdoors.

subject_plans.mathematics: Grade 6 scope (Common Core domains 6.RP, 6.NS, 6.EE, 6.G, 6.SP): ratios and proportional reasoning; dividing fractions by fractions and operating with the full set of rational numbers, including negatives; writing and evaluating algebraic expressions and equations; area, surface area, and volume; an introduction to statistical thinking (measures of center, variability). Mater Amabilis approach: the student justifies each step, not just performs it.
subject_plans.art_music: Composer: Frédéric Chopin — Romantic-era piano music, intimate and emotionally direct (a nocturne is an ideal entry point). Artist: Katsushika Hokusai — bold, graphic Japanese woodblock prints (The Great Wave off Kanagawa), a deliberate step outside the Western tradition studied so far. Poet: Emily Dickinson — compact, puzzling poems that reward re-reading and pattern-finding, well matched to the Logic Stage.
subject_plans.language_arts: Grade 6: literary analysis vocabulary is introduced (simile, metaphor, plot structure, characterization) and used in discussing that term's living books. Multi-paragraph written composition is now expected as the norm. Grammar review shifts toward how the student actually uses language in their own writing, rather than isolated drills.
subject_plans.morning_time: Scripture with real discussion of its meaning and context, a hymn or folk song, and Dickinson for poetry. Connect briefly to this term's history (the founding era, or Kim's India) when it fits naturally.
